speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is the 6th of December 1995. The survivor is Hanns Saenger. The interviewer is Heather Rushovich in Johannesburg, South Africa, in the English language. I'm Heather Rushovich. Today's the 6th of December 1995. I'll be conducting interview with Hanns Saenger in Hyde Park, Johannesburg, South Africa. Can you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanns Herman Saenger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-A-E-N-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and was this your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 16Th of August 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does that make your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: What city and country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Berlin, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did your family live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father must have got there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] around about 1902 or '03.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he met your mother there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he met my mother there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many members were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gerald. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- so there were four of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents, what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Irma, and my father's name was Kurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's parents was-- were Julius and Hermine Eisenhardt. And they came from Alsace-Lorraine. And my father's parents were Herman Saenger-- he came from Inowrazlaw in Poland-- and my grandmother's name was Johanna Mushke. And they got married in Chelmno in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have anyone else that lived with you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, well, only a nanny when we were young, and then a governess until we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, before-- can you just lift that little barrel?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the role of the governess and nanny in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nanny brought us up as babies. And the governess took over from her as we grew-- as I grew older.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents get involved, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I was aware of. Obviously, they were in the background. But basically, it was done by the staff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home? What your house looked like, what you remember of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very large apartment, about 10 or 12 rooms. Very luxurious-- for the times. And it was centrally situated in the residential area of Berlin-- it's called Charlottenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what-- what do you remember your daily life like to be, before everything started? What did you do on a day to day basis with your family, with your brother, activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, I only remember the time when I went to school. Quite frankly, I don't remember going to the-- to primary school. I remember going to the gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was government school. It was the Kaiser Friedrich Realgymnasium. It was a very well-known government school. And school was in the mornings. And the afternoons, there was no-- no such thing as sports at school. In the afternoon and in winter, we went skating. In summer, we played tennis. We visited friends. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a very peaceful existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you observe? Or how did you practice your orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in a moderate way. I mean, we kept Shabbat, we kept every Yom Tov. And my father came from a very orthodox background. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother was-- her parents were less observant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to keep kosher, in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother kept kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember the shul's name and the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi's name was Rabbi Dr. Selamonski. And the shul was called the Luetzestrasse-- which is a very large, downtown shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you involved in the community? And with the whole Jewish community life in the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was a gabbai of the shul, as long as I can remember. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the occasional shiurim, probably once in ten days. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to [NON-ENGLISH] shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: An anointer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe the Jewish community in which you grew up? Just the size, the economic activity, and just the whole organization, about the whole Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a very structured, well-run community. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] If my memory serves me right, Germany, at that time, had something like just under 500,000 Jews. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the synagogues were very large. They housed between 1,500 and 2,000 members each. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish life was really organized, well-structured.

speaker: interviewer
text: Organized in what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were educational programs. There were shiurim there were-- for adults, for children, for teenagers. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews didn't really mix with non-Jews, socially.

speaker: interviewer
text: The reason being?

speaker: interviewee
text: You felt more at home with your own people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the Jewish community quite a wealthy one?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very wealthy one. I mean generally speaking, it was wealthy community.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you all lived in the same neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were a number of Jewish neighborhoods. I mean, I'm describing Berlin-- I don't really know what-- what Jewish life was like in the country, but it did exist in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever go to Jewish youth camps as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never went. I don't think-- I don't know whether they existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a very wealthy man. And he made his money from 1921 to '27, during the inflation in Germany when he [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] instinctively knew how to make the most of the conditions. He exchanged the daily takings of his stores into gold coins daily, except on shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it closed on shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think the stores were closed. He didn't work. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he came through that period of the inflation in Germany a very wealthy man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he sell?

speaker: interviewee
text: He sold men's hats and caps, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many shops did he have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know. But somewhere around 60 or 70 stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had people managing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever work in any of the stores?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in a store every [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] free day that I had, I used to spend there. My job was to put the hat-- the sold hats into a paper bag. I remember that very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother, did he become involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: The reason being?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother did she ever work in the stores?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother lived a very spoiled life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was not a well woman from 1928 onwards, and needed a lot of care. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She also came from a wealthy background, and she never had to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your mother die?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died of cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1934, in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe relationships between Jews and non-Jews of your time? Even though you say you weren't friendly with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it must be realized that in Germany, Jews were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different in look to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Jews look?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews looked-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] well, the Jewish look was totally different to the Germanic look, which was the Aryan look, like the Swedes and the Norwegians and Danish people. And in-- one of the things that made Hitler's rise easy, as far as anti-Semitism was concerned, the Jews stood out in a crowd. People knew you were a Jew by looking at you because you looked different. This is not the case in countries like France, or Spain, or any of the-- Italy. But it was the case in Austria, and in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you feel that it did put you at an immediate disadvantage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many Jewish people were at your school or in your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I would guess--

speaker: interviewer
text: Percentage wise.

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps 10%, 12% of the-- of the class.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were only friendly with the Jewish people at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-Semitism at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not directly. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you were made to feel different.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the teachers or by other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: By both.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember any specific examples?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was indirect. But it was inferred.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you felt it, even as a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you experience any anti-Semitism outside the school? If you can be very specific.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the same thing. I mean, you-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] or perhaps my father coming from Poland, we were more sensitive to anti-Semitism than Jews that were of German extraction, because they hadn't lived through any anti-Semitic times. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it's best illustrated by the fact that my father said, in 1928, that Germany's finished for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made him say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler came out of prison, and published his book And he saw, very clearly, that anti-Semitism was going to be played up by Hitler. And the Germans would swallow whatever he said. And he started closing his stores, selling them-- usually to the managers of the respective stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: For this reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he felt that he was doing the right thing at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. By 1932, he had sold all but about six or eight of his stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he prepared. And when he told the other members of the Jewish community, did they agree with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that stage, he kept it to himself. But his general attitude was well known. And people-- the average German Jew did not agree with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did non-Jews by from his shops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there wasn't a problem there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ashamed to be Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. I was proud to be one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you belong to any organizations, like a political or cultural organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that age, what were your goals, as a young person, looking towards the future, not knowing what was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw myself taking over my father's business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were looking forward to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Hitler's rise to power in 1933 affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Hitler came in February, '33. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the year prior to him coming in, it was quite obvious that he was going to come in. Hindenburg was going to appoint him chancellor. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was well-prepared, and the German nation reacts to military music, and marching, and clicking their heels, and flags hanging and so on. Everyone wore an armband with a swastika on it. And the posters started appearing widely-- don't buy from Jews, or this is a Jewish shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they listened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They smashed Jewish shop windows. To give you an example, my father's business was called English Club before World War I. And they smashed his stores, because they thought it was-- they were English-owned. So he changed the name to Deutsche Hut-Vertrieb. And when the Nazis came in, they smashed the shops, because they were called Deutsche, and he was a Jew. So he changed it for the second time. And he changed it to Der Hut-Vertrieb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means 'the hat distributor.' And it meant he could retain the logo of the organization. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he couldn't do right. I mean, it-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anything that was called German could not be Jewish. They wouldn't allow that. The Nazis wouldn't allow it. And they started burning books. They started boycotting Jewish shops-- before Hitler became chancellor. So that when he did become chancellor, things didn't really change. The pace merely accelerated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did the shop survive if most of the Germans boycotted buying there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They suffered. I mean, the Jews, the Jewish businesses suffered, definitely. But the earlier part of Hitler's reign, the time that I was in Germany, it was nothing compared to probably from 1937, '38 onwards, when he really-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when he had published his final solution, which was to eliminate the Jews totally-- Jews, gypsies, homosexuals, and cripples. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those were all to be eliminated. And that's when he-- what I would-- the concentration camps, at first, were labor camps. And they became concentration camps. And finally, they became death camps. And in fact, Chelmno was the first camp in Poland, where gas was used.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about that at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but we learned it. Well, we were no longer in Germany then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the Germans were breaking into shops and making sure that they were boycotting, how did-- when you were still in Germany-- how did you feel? How did your family feel? And how did the Jewish community feel about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was saw little of it, because I was in a camp from April '33 until June '33. And the day that I came out, we left Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll get to it later. But when-- before you left Germany, did your father make any attempt to secure his assets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, his plans were to leave Germany in 1932. By 1932, he had moved all his money officially, and through the official sources-- the banks-- into Switzerland. And in 1932, the then finance minister Bruening brought out exchange control, which forbade any resident in Germany to have accounts outside Germany. And my mother's health had deteriorated. And she was operated in 1932. And that's when they discovered that she had cancer. And the doctors advised my father that he could not move her. He would have to stay near the hospital where the professor who operated was. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] situation became so dangerous for my father that he said he must bring the money back. He can't live in Germany, having transferred his money out officially through the banks. It was known that he had done so. So he brought his entire fortune back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he made arrangements that should he ever leave, the French consul would move whatever cash he gave him for 25% into France.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- at the end, after you had left, did you have any more cash left in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father took all the cash that he had been able to accumulate from 1928 onward, and kept it in numerous private bank accounts. And he kept transferring from one to the other. And on every transfer he pulled some money out into cash, which he put into safes. And so he had the larger portion of his money was in cash. And he was able to move that. But his fixed assets, of course, he lost-- he left behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fixed assets, as in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buildings, our apartment, the real estate that he owned, the factories that he owned. Those he just lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever know what became of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he refused to ever put a claim into-- he was not going to deal with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just because of what had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he said in principle he was not prepared to deal with Germans. When he died, I was an executor of his estate, together with a German Jewish lawyer. And we decided that it was foolish not to put in claims. Unfortunately, I never found his records of where his properties were situated. But I knew some of the names. And we were able through-- with the help of a German lawyer, after the war, to trace a number of properties. And for those I got compensation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Adequate compensation?

speaker: interviewee
text: It can never be adequate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only for what they were worth? No, nothing like it. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The-- the values that they paid out were minimal compared to the real value. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they're doing it now with East Germany. I've got claims running in East Germany. [BACKGROUND NOISE] What claims are you-- what you got in East Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's some real estate in East Germany that we were able to trace. And we got a judgement in 1962 from the West German courts, to prove that the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] price that the Nazis paid for that building, or those buildings, was exactly the amount of money of the tax that Jews had to pay in order to leave Germany-- and had no relation at all to the value of the buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the court then gave me a ruling that this was an illegal sale, and I was to be refunded. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When East Germany joined with Germany, the laws in East Germany were withdrawn, and the West German laws applied. So now the East German claims are being processed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you getting any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hopefully.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still waiting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you--are you expected to get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's on the lip of the gods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever called up to the army before you left Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was 14. When I was taken to camp, I was 13 and 1/2 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in that age, did you know about Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through the media or through your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the media, through the Nazi papers, through the discussions at home-- discussions wherever you went. I mean, you only heard Hitler or his-- his-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] senior officer speaking out in public meetings. And you couldn't help but know what was going on.
